title: Quantitative Contact Big Fiber Theorem
speaker: Jun Zhang
affiliation: University of Science and Technology China
abstract: In this talk, we will present a proof of contact big fiber theorem, based on invariants read off from contact Hamiltonian Floer homology. The theorem concludes that any contact involutive map on a Liouville fillable contact manifold admits at least one fiber that is non-displaceable, under the condition that the Liouville filling has non-vanishing symplectic homology. Note that this result was also proved recently by Sun-Uljarevic-Varolgunes, based on symplectic homology with compact support. Along with the proof, we will also propose a definition of partial contact quasi-state (and contact quasi-measure), serving as a contact analog to Entov-Polterovich’s quasi-state machinery in the symplectic setting. This talk is based on joint work with Igor Uljarevic.

proof_stages.label: contact Hamiltonian and alpha-involutive setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes contact-Hamiltonian conventions, the contact/Jacobi bracket and strictness, then defines alpha-involutive maps whose components and the constant function 1 commute in this bracket.

proof_stages.label: symplectic homology hypothesis and big-fiber statement
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing Viterbo-style symplectic homology, continuation maps, localization, and examples of vanishing/nonvanishing, the main theorem is stated: if the filling has nonzero symplectic homology, every alpha-involutive map has a nondisplaceable fiber.

proof_stages.label: contact Hamiltonian Floer homology and twisted persistence
proof_stages.summary: The proof is based on contact Hamiltonian Floer homology, defined by Floer homology for Hamiltonians asymptotic to r h_t, with a twist by eta sharp h that removes action-filtration ambiguity and yields a persistence module over a partially ordered parameter set.

proof_stages.label: spectral invariant from the unit
proof_stages.summary: A spectral number c_alpha(h) associated to the unit in symplectic homology is extracted from the persistence module, and its normalization, stability, triangle-type, strict-invariance, and error estimates are established.

proof_stages.label: homogenized partial contact quasi-state
proof_stages.summary: The spectral invariant is homogenized to zeta_alpha, and its key properties are recorded: normalization on constants, monotonicity/stability, subadditivity on commuting strict Hamiltonians, positive homogeneity, and vanishing on displaceably supported strict Hamiltonians.

proof_stages.label: quasi-measure on closed subsets
proof_stages.summary: Using zeta_alpha, the speaker defines a set function tau_alpha on closed subsets and proves the properties needed for the covering argument, including tau_alpha(M)=1, monotonicity, subadditivity for alpha-subordinate collections, and vanishing on displaceable sets.

proof_stages.label: covering contradiction proving the big-fiber theorem
proof_stages.summary: Assuming every fiber is displaceable, the image is covered by finitely many neighborhoods whose preimages are displaceable and alpha-subordinate, forcing tau_alpha(M) to be both 1 and at most a sum of zeros, a contradiction.

examples.name: standard contact sphere moment map
examples.note: The toric moment map on the standard contact sphere makes the definition of an alpha-involutive map concrete and is later cited to show why the nonvanishing symplectic-homology hypothesis is necessary.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the explicit moment map on the standard contact sphere and checks strictness/contact vector fields and alpha-involutivity for its components

passes.reason: the toric moment map on the maximally symmetric standard contact sphere is used to show the conclusion can fail without the nonzero symplectic-homology hypothesis, since all fibers are displaceable
The standard toric moment map on the maximally symmetric contact sphere is used to show the big-fiber conclusion can fail when symplectic homology vanishes.
The standard toric contact sphere moment map is maximally symmetric, and it is used as the negative case where all fibers are displaceable once the nonzero symplectic-homology hypothesis is absent.

the standard toric contact sphere moment map is maximally regular/symmetric and is used to show the big-fiber conclusion fails without the nonzero symplectic-homology hypothesis, since all its fibers are displaceable.

The standard contact sphere with its toric moment map is a maximally regular symmetric model, and it is used to show the big-fiber conclusion fails without the nonzero symplectic-homology hypothesis because all fibers are displaceable.
The standard toric contact sphere moment map is maximally regular/symmetric and is used to show the nonzero symplectic-homology hypothesis is necessary, since all its fibers are displaceable.

The standard toric moment map on the contact sphere is used to show the big-fiber conclusion can fail when the symplectic-homology hypothesis is absent, and the object is the highly symmetric standard sphere/torus-action model.
The standard toric moment map on the maximally symmetric contact sphere is used to show the big-fiber conclusion can fail without the nonzero symplectic-homology hypothesis.
Used to show the big-fiber conclusion fails without the nonzero symplectic-homology hypothesis, and the standard contact sphere is a maximally regular/symmetric model.
The standard contact sphere moment map is used as the highly symmetric toric model where all fibers are displaceable, showing the nonzero symplectic-homology hypothesis is necessary.
The standard toric contact sphere moment map is used to show the big-fiber conclusion can fail when symplectic homology vanishes, and the standard sphere is maximally symmetric.
the standard toric contact sphere moment map is used to show the big-fiber conclusion can fail when symplectic homology vanishes, and the standard sphere is maximally regular/symmetric

examples.name: Brieskorn/Maslov-type contact sphere example
examples.note: The Brieskorn manifold with its explicit functions supplies a non-toric alpha-involutive system and contrasts with the standard sphere through nondisplaceability detected by symplectic homology.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker gives the Brieskorn sphere by equations, counts the dimension/codimension, writes the contact form and explicit functions, and states the involutivity check for this instance

passes.reason: the Brieskorn contact-sphere construction is a systematic non-toric positive example of an alpha-involutive map, with non-displaceable-fiber behavior rather than a failure case
The Brieskorn contact sphere is a nontrivial member of a systematic family used as a positive illustrative example with non-displaceable fibers, not as a sporadic exception or refutation.

The Brieskorn/Ustilovsky-type contact sphere is a non-toric but systematic family example used positively to exhibit an alpha-involutive map with nondisplaceability phenomena, not to refute the theorem.
the Brieskorn sphere Sigma(p,2,ldots,2) is a structured infinite-family contact-sphere example used positively as a nontrivial alpha-involutive system, not to refute a claim.

The Brieskorn/Ustilovsky-type contact sphere is a nontrivial member of a systematic Brieskorn family rather than a sporadic exception, and it is presented as a positive contrasting example with non-displaceable fibers, not as an obstruction to the theorem.

The Brieskorn \(\Sigma(p,2,\ldots,2)\) contact-sphere construction is presented as a non-toric family example with a nondisplaceable fiber, not as a sporadic classification exception or adversarial counterexample.

The Brieskorn-type contact sphere is presented as a nontrivial positive source of an alpha-involutive map with nondisplaceable fibers, not as a failure case; it belongs to a systematic Brieskorn family rather than a sporadic exception, though its many equal quadratic coordinates give it notable symmetry.
The Brieskorn-family contact sphere supplies a non-toric alpha-involutive example with nondisplaceable fibers, not an adversarial disproof, and is not a sporadic classification exception.
Used as a nontrivial Brieskorn contact-sphere example of an alpha-involutive system with rigid fibers, but it belongs to a systematic family rather than being sporadic or extremal.
The Brieskorn contact-sphere family is presented as a positive non-toric alpha-involutive example with a non-displaceable fiber, not as a sporadic classification exception or failure case.
The Brieskorn/Ustilovsky-type contact sphere supplies a nontrivial positive alpha-involutive example with a non-displaceable fiber, not an obstruction to the theorem.
the Brieskorn/Ustilovsky-type contact sphere is presented as a non-toric positive example of an alpha-involutive system with nondisplaceable fibers, not as a sporadic or extremal object
